questions.scenario: A browser opens `https://app.example.com/dashboard`. DNS returns `203.0.113.25`, the host route sends traffic through `10.0.0.1`, TCP port `443` accepts a connection, TLS succeeds, and Nginx forwards the request to `127.0.0.1:3000`. Which sequence best describes how the request advanced through the stack?

options.text: TCP selected the public IP, DNS opened port 443, Ethernet validated the certificate, and HTTP chose the gateway
DNS chose the local MAC address, IP selected the Nginx process, TCP resolved the hostname, and HTTP created the route
DNS resolved the name, IP selected a route, TCP opened the port conversation, TLS protected it, and HTTP carried the application request
Ethernet kept the same MAC destination across every router, TCP selected the subnet, and Nginx performed the DNS lookup

questions.explanation: The request moves from name resolution to IP routing, then TCP connection setup, TLS, HTTP, and the proxy handoff. The other choices assign names, routes, ports, and certificates to layers that do not own those jobs. [RFC 1122](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1122) describes the layered internet host model, while [RFC 9293](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9293.html) defines TCP connection behavior.

questions.scenario:
A packet review for one HTTPS request shows these fields:

| Evidence | Value |
| --- | --- |
| HTTP | `GET /orders` and `Host: api.example.com` |
| TCP | source port `53142`, destination port `443` |
| IP | `10.0.0.42` to `203.0.113.25` |
| Ethernet | laptop MAC to gateway MAC |

The next router replaces the Ethernet header before forwarding the packet. Which explanation is accurate?

options.text: The router must also replace the HTTP Host header because every layer is rewritten at each hop
Each layer adds metadata for its own delivery job; the local MAC addresses can change at a hop while the IP destination and inner HTTP request remain meaningful
The TCP source port is the physical interface identifier, while the destination MAC identifies the application process
The IP header is removed permanently at the first router, leaving only an Ethernet frame for the rest of the path

questions.explanation: Encapsulation lets HTTP, TCP, IP, and the local link carry different delivery information. Routers replace the link-layer frame for the next local hop, but they forward the IP packet without turning MAC addresses into application or process identifiers. [RFC 1122](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1122) explains this layered host behavior.

questions.scenario:
A deployment is healthy from one subnet but unreachable from a new worker subnet. DNS resolves correctly. On an affected worker:

```text
$ ip route get 10.80.12.25
10.80.12.25 via 10.80.0.1 dev eth0 src 10.80.0.44

$ traceroute 10.80.12.25
 1  10.80.0.1  1.1 ms
 2  * * *
 3  * * *
```

Which investigation should come first?

options.text: Inspect the application route handler because DNS resolution proves all lower layers are healthy
Renew the TLS certificate because traceroute suppresses certificate errors as asterisks
Check whether the worker can resolve the destination's MAC directly, even though the destination is in another subnet
Inspect routing and filtering beyond the first gateway, while confirming with destination probes because traceroute asterisks can also mean that intermediate devices suppress probe replies

questions.explanation: The worker has an address and a route through its gateway, so routing and filtering beyond that gateway are appropriate first checks. The asterisks do not by themselves locate the fault because routers may forward traffic while suppressing TTL-expired replies. Confirm with a probe that resembles the real destination flow and inspect route or policy evidence. DNS success does not prove the IP path, and an off-subnet host does not ARP directly for the destination. [Paris traceroute](https://paris-traceroute.net/about/) explains common traceroute interpretation limits.

questions.scenario:
Two database nodes should share VLAN 44. `db-a` uses `10.44.8.10/24` and `db-b` uses `10.44.8.20/24`. From `db-a`:

```text
$ ip neigh show 10.44.8.20
10.44.8.20 dev eth0 FAILED

$ ip route get 10.44.8.20
10.44.8.20 dev eth0 src 10.44.8.10
```

What does this evidence support most strongly?

options.text: A Layer 2 problem such as a VLAN mismatch or failed neighbor discovery, because same-subnet delivery needs the peer's MAC address
A missing default route, because all same-subnet traffic must pass through the gateway
A closed database TCP port, because a failed neighbor entry proves the server sent a reset
An HTTP application failure, because neighbor discovery runs after the database returns a response

questions.explanation: The route says the peer is directly connected, so `db-a` must resolve `db-b` to a local MAC address before sending the frame. The failed neighbor entry points to the local link, not the default gateway, TCP listener, or application protocol. [RFC 1122](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1122) describes link-layer handling beneath IP.

questions.scenario:
A capture was taken while a client opened an HTTPS connection:

```text
12:01:10.100 IP 10.0.0.42.53142 > 203.0.113.25.443: Flags [S], seq 100, length 0
12:01:10.132 IP 203.0.113.25.443 > 10.0.0.42.53142: Flags [S.], seq 200, ack 101, length 0
12:01:10.132 IP 10.0.0.42.53142 > 203.0.113.25.443: Flags [.], ack 201, length 0
12:01:10.150 IP 10.0.0.42.53142 > 203.0.113.25.443: Flags [P.], length 517
```

What is the strongest conclusion before examining the encrypted payload?

options.text: The server returned a successful HTTP response because any packet with `[P.]` contains an HTTP 200 status
The TLS certificate is valid because the server sent a SYN-ACK from port 443
The TCP three-way handshake completed and application data began flowing, so basic IP reachability and the transport path worked
The destination MAC stayed unchanged across the entire route because the IP endpoints stayed unchanged

questions.explanation: SYN, SYN-ACK, and ACK complete the TCP handshake, and `[P.]` shows that data followed. The capture does not prove the HTTP status or certificate validity because HTTPS payloads are encrypted, and MAC addresses can change at every routed hop. [RFC 9293](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9293.html) defines the TCP handshake and states, while the [tcpdump manual](https://www.tcpdump.org/manpages/tcpdump.1.html) documents capture output.

questions.scenario: A checkout outage produces failures at several sites, and the incident commander must route each case without claiming more than the evidence proves. One host reports `NO-CARRIER` and `state DOWN`. At a second site, captures show repeated TCP SYN packets to port 443 with no SYN-ACK. A third client completes the connection but rejects a certificate whose name does not match the hostname. At the final site, Nginx returns 502 and records `connect() failed (111: Connection refused) while connecting to upstream`. Which assignments are justified? Select all that apply.

options.text: Send the SYN-only case directly to the application team because the capture is sufficient to distinguish an HTTP rejection from network filtering
Send case amber to the physical or interface owner because the local link has no carrier
Send the certificate-name case to the routing team first because a completed TLS connection still indicates an unresolved next-hop problem
Send case blue to the transport-path and service-listener owners because the handshake is incomplete, but the capture alone does not distinguish filtering from an unavailable listener or return path
Send case red to the proxy or upstream service owner because public HTTP reached Nginx and its internal connection was refused

questions.explanation: No carrier is a local interface problem. Repeated SYN packets without a completed handshake keep the investigation below HTTP, but they do not prove one narrow cause: filtering, asymmetric return routing, or listener-side behavior can all require checks. A `502` with an upstream refusal shows that Nginx answered the client but could not connect to its backend. The certificate mismatch belongs to TLS validation. [RFC 9293](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9293.html) defines TCP setup, and [RFC 9110](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110) defines HTTP response semantics.

questions.scenario:
A user reports that checkout is down. The first checks show:

```text
$ dig +short checkout.example.com
203.0.113.40

$ nc -vz checkout.example.com 443
Connection to checkout.example.com (203.0.113.40) 443 port [tcp/https] succeeded!

$ curl -I https://checkout.example.com/ready
curl: (60) SSL certificate problem: certificate has expired
```

What should the on-call do next?

options.text: Change the DNS record because certificate expiry means the resolver returned no address
Open the network firewall because a successful TCP connection means port 443 is blocked
Change the default gateway because the client could not reach the server IP
Inspect and renew the certificate served for the hostname because name resolution and TCP connectivity are already proven

questions.explanation: DNS returned the expected address and TCP port `443` accepted a connection, so changing DNS, the route, or the firewall ignores evidence that those stages worked. The failure occurs during TLS certificate validation, so the next check is the certificate selected for this hostname. [RFC 9110](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110) describes HTTPS semantics above the established transport connection.
